title: Reclaiming Your Gaze: Eye Contact Practice After Long Suppression
dek: When you’ve spent years shrinking your gaze, avoiding the raw edge of connection, the simple act of holding eye contact can feel like standing under a storm. Your sternocleidomastoid tightens, your throat clenches, and the jaw remembers every time it folded away from confrontation. This is not pathology, this is biology waking up - an invitation to meet yourself and others with fierce, unashamed presence.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Weight of Looking Away


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T01:52:43.083Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

It’s Tuesday morning, the kind of gray light that slips in through the narrow blinds and pools in the corners of a fluorescent-lit office. The air hums with the brittle chill of air conditioning, a faint scent of printer toner mixing with stale coffee. You sit at a desk, your back straight but not quite rooted, your shoulders pulled forward in a habitual curl that has become your armor. The clock ticks, steady and unrelenting, a metronome to the tension winding through your body. You catch the eyes of the person across from you - briefly - and then, as if a silent command has gone off inside your chest, you look away. 

That break in connection isn’t just a choice. Your suboccipital muscles, those tiny but fierce bundles at the base of your skull, tighten like the drawstrings of a sack. It’s a subtle clench, a coil of resistance that pulls your head slightly forward and down, as if bowing to an invisible force. The sternocleidomastoid, running from your collarbone up to behind your ear, activates in tandem, turning your head just enough to avoid the confrontation of gaze. This isn’t just an act of avoidance; it’s a physical response that your body has learned over years of silencing. Your breath shifts, becoming shallower, a flickering flame barely catching in the upper chest. The ribs don’t expand fully; the diaphragm hesitates, flattened beneath the weight of a stomach that drops in a knot of uncertainty and something heavier - fear, shame, a memory of being told fury was ugly.

You can almost feel the fascia, that web of connective tissue threading through your neck and shoulders, tightening like a corset, pulling everything inward. Your trapezius muscle, spanning from your neck down your back, stiffens, anchoring you in a posture that says, “Don’t be seen. Don’t make waves.” This is not a simple habit. It’s a survival mechanism etched into your muscles and nerves, a somatic echo of long suppression. The body remembers what the mind tries to forget.

In this moment, the air tastes faintly metallic - perhaps the residue of stress hormones coursing through your veins, or the remnants of last night’s herbal tea steeped from dried chamomile and valerian root, meant to soothe but barely scratching the surface of your held tension. You notice the slight ache behind your eyes, a dull pressure that builds with every glance you avoid. Your jaw clenches automatically, masseter muscles tightening like twin knots of fabric twisted too tight. The mouth remains closed, lips pressed together in a line that stifles expression. The body knows this is safer, quieter.

You want to meet their eyes, to hold your ground, but the sensation of looking away is so ingrained it feels like breathing. The suboccipital muscles release only when you allow the head to lift and turn back, but doing so feels like stepping into a cold rain after years of hiding under shelter. The room’s fluorescent light flickers overhead, casting a sterile glow on your skin, making you acutely aware of every tiny movement, every pulse of blood beneath the thin skin of your neck. Your heart beats a rapid rhythm, not quite panic but close - a reminder that this is not just about eye contact. It’s about reclaiming space in your own body, about undoing the muscle memory of shame and silence.

This is the somatic reality of avoiding eye contact after long suppression. It’s not a mere social discomfort; it’s the body’s way of speaking a language learned through years of being told your visibility was dangerous. Your muscles carry the history, the fascia holds the memory, and your breath hesitates at the threshold of vulnerability. This is not pathology, this is biology. And to begin looking someone in the eye again is to begin unraveling a physical story that has been written into your flesh.

## The Biology of the Gaze: How Your Body Reads the Eyes

When your eyes lock with someone else’s, it’s never just a matter of seeing. The body knows this - deep in the tissues, in the quiet conversations between nerves and muscles. Your gaze is a language older than words, older than culture, older than the polite smiles you learned to wear like armor. It is primal, raw, and unyielding. The muscles around your eyes, the tiny orbicularis oculi, don’t just contract to blink; they twitch in anticipation or recoil in defense. Your nervous system - especially the vagus nerve and the sympathetic chain - fires messages that ripple through your body, preparing you to react.

Look at the masseter muscle right now, clenching softly at the edges of your jaw. This muscle, which you use to chew, also tightens when you feel threatened. It’s the body’s way of bracing, like armor closing over a vulnerable place. When someone holds your gaze too long, your masseter and temporalis muscles may tighten without you realizing it, a silent warning signal from your nervous system. This isn’t about conscious thought or social conditioning; this is biology. Your body remembers what your jaw remembers: that intense eye contact, especially from a stranger or an adversary, could be the prelude to a predator’s strike or an assertion of dominance.

The gaze can feel like a predator’s lock, an unblinking stare that insists on your submission or your fight. Your autonomic nervous system interprets it as a challenge, and your body responds accordingly. Your psoas muscle, nestled deep in your abdomen, contracts. This muscle, often called the “fight or flight” muscle, tenses up as your body prepares to either flee or face down the threat. When the psoas tightens, it pulls your spine into a protective curve, your breath shallows, and your heart rate quickens. You might feel your chest tighten or a hollow ache in your lower back, even if the person across from you only made eye contact for a moment.

When you were taught that your fury was ugly, you were learning to mask a fundamental biological response. The flicker of anger that rises when someone’s gaze holds you hostage is not a flaw in your character. It is your nervous system calling out to be heard, your muscles bracing for something your mind may not yet understand. The masseter remembers every time you clenched your jaw to keep quiet. The psoas remembers every time you held your breath because speaking felt too dangerous. This is not pathology; this is biology.

In traditional Chinese medicine, the eyes are tied directly to the Liver and Gallbladder meridians, organs responsible for the smooth flow of qi and the regulation of anger. When your gaze meets someone else’s and the tension rises, it’s not just emotional - it’s energetic and muscular and deeply physical. Your body constricts, your fascia tightens around your eyes and jaw, and your nervous system shifts into a state of alert. The body’s response to eye contact is a complex dance of muscles and nerves, a choreography written into your cells long before you learned to smile politely.

At 3 a.m., when the house is quiet and the night air smells damp with the scent of rain-soaked earth, your body might replay those moments of locked gazes. You might feel the subtle tightening of the masseter, the deep coil of the psoas, the pull of your breath up into your throat. The body remembers. Your nervous system does not lie. It holds the truth of your fury, your fear, your fierce desire to exist without apology.

So when someone tells you to “calm down” or “soften your gaze,” it’s not just a request for politeness. It’s a demand to silence your biology, to dismiss the language your muscles and nerves speak fluently. Your body’s response to eye contact is a map of survival, a record of what you’ve endured and what you continue to endure. The jaw remembers. The psoas remembers. And you? You are learning to listen.

## How the Body Holds On: Trauma’s Architecture and Herbal Allies

You know the moment well - the way your trapezius, that thick muscle spanning the back of your neck and shoulders, tenses so tightly by dusk that it feels like a clenched fist pressed against your spine. This is no accident, no mere habit; it’s the language of your body speaking in muscle and fascia, a record etched deep by the years of holding what you weren't allowed to feel. The jaw remembers this too - masseter muscles clenched in silent fury when you were told your rage was ugly, a betrayal of your femininity. This is not pathology, this is biology. The body knows trauma as a physical reality before the mind can name it.

Bessel van der Kolk, a pioneer in trauma research, has shown us what the body does when it faces the unspeakable. Trauma doesn’t just lodge in the mind as a memory; it lodges in the body’s very architecture. The nervous system, designed to protect you with its swift fight-or-flight responses, becomes a fortress that never lowers its walls. The sympathetic nervous system, your body’s accelerator, revs constantly in the background, primed for a threat that might have passed years ago but still lives in your muscle fibers. The parasympathetic system, your brake and rest mechanism, struggles to engage fully. This imbalance rewires your entire physiology.

At 3 AM on a cold, damp night when the wind presses against the windowpanes, you might feel the subtle tremble of your diaphragm, shallow breaths that betray the tension held in your solar plexus. Van der Kolk emphasizes that trauma survivors often get stuck in a state of hyperarousal or shutdown, neither of which allows the nervous system to complete its protective response. When your body can’t finish the defensive sequence - whether that’s fight, flight, or freeze - it stores the energy like unspent electricity in the tissues. This is why your body feels frozen, why your rage stays lodged in the jaw and your fear tightens the belly.

Traditional herbal medicine understands this deeply. Milky Oats (Avena sativa), with its gentle yet profound tonic effect on the nervous system, supports the rebuilding of resilience. It’s not the kind of quick fix that dulls your senses or blunts your feelings; rather, it nourishes the long fibers of your spinal erectors and calms the jittery nerves at their roots. You can smell its sweet, grassy aroma as you prepare it, a scent that feels like the first morning light after a night of storm.

Skullcap (Scutellaria lateriflora) walks alongside Milky Oats, offering its nervine qualities to ease the tremors and tension in your occipital region, that base of your skull where your neck muscles attach and where so much tension accumulates. This herb is bitter and earthy, a medicine for the restless mind and the muscles that clench in anxiety. It reminds you that the body can soften, that the jaw can unclench, and that the breath can deepen into the belly.

Wood Betony (Stachys officinalis) is another ally, traditionally known to settle the nervous system and open the chest, releasing the fascia that tightens around the ribs and restricts the breath. Its subtle, minty aroma fills the air, inviting the body to remember how to expand, how to hold space for emotions without collapsing under their weight.

These herbs do not erase the trauma, nor do they promise quick relief. They are serious medicine, slow and steady, working with your body's own wisdom to restore balance. They honor the fact that your nervous system is not a broken machine but a living landscape shaped by your history. The body is constantly adapting, and these plants are a way to support those adaptations - not by forcing change but by providing the nourishment your tissues need to remember safety.

You might find yourself sipping a warm infusion of Milky Oats and Skullcap after a long day when your trapezius muscles scream at you from their tight coil, or carrying a small tincture of Wood Betony in your bag during that restless night when the diaphragm refuses to relax. The scent of these herbs reminds you of your own resilience - the way your body, though shaped by trauma, still holds the capacity to heal.

Remember, this is not about ignoring your rage or smoothing it over with sweetness. It is about meeting the fury in your body with respect and care, understanding that every clenched muscle, every shallow breath, is a chapter in your story. These herbs are the quiet witnesses, the steady hands, the medicine that reminds the nervous system it can lower its defenses and breathe again.

## The Weight of Looking Away: Anger Held in the Diaphragm

You know the moment well: a flicker of heat in your belly, a tightening just beneath the ribs where your diaphragm lives - this dome of muscle that separates your lungs from your gut. It’s the part of your body that knows your fury before your mind can name it. You feel it rise, a pressure that wants to expand, to push outward, but instead you swallow it down. The jaw clenches, the throat tightens, and the breath shortens not by accident but by habit. This is not pathology, this is biology.

Karla McLaren’s work on emotional intelligence reminds us that emotions like anger are not enemies to be vanquished or hidden away. They are signals with messages, textures, and rhythms. Anger, in particular, is the body's fierce sentinel, a guardian of boundaries and a fire that says, “No more.” But you, like so many women before you, were taught that fury is ugly, dangerous, and best kept secret. The cultural weight of that lesson presses into your diaphragm, pressing down the very muscle that wants to rise and open with the force of your anger.

Your ancestors carry this same burden. Generations of women who learned to look away when a boundary was crossed, who learned to soften their edges so as not to disturb the fragile peace. The stories you were told, the silences you inherited, are held in the connective tissue around your ribcage. The fascia remembers every moment you swallowed your rage, every time you told yourself to be “nice” or “quiet.” The body knows this weight, and it shows up as a dull ache or a sudden pang just below your sternum, where the diaphragm contracts against the ribs. That sensation is the body’s way of saying, “I am here, I am not invisible.”

You might notice this in the late hours, perhaps around 2 or 3 a.m., when the world is still and your breath becomes shallow. The cool night air seeps in through a cracked window, carrying the scent of damp earth and fallen leaves. It’s in these quiet moments that the body’s memory becomes loudest. The diaphragm tightens reflexively with each breath, as though holding back a tide. Your belly might feel bruised, your lower ribs tender from the constant tension of holding in what wants to be expressed. This is the physical echo of a cultural command to look away, to close your mouth, to hide your anger.

But the jaw remembers, too. The masseter muscle clenches involuntarily, grinding teeth as if to keep the fury locked in. Your tongue presses against the roof of your mouth, a subtle but firm blockade to the words you never say. This is not weakness - it is survival. It is the body negotiating between the need to protect yourself and the need to remain safe in a world that long told you anger was a fault, not a force.

When you finally learn to honor your anger, you are not just changing your mind; you are changing your biology. You are allowing the diaphragm to drop fully, to expand the lungs and press into the belly. You are letting the heat rise without apology. This is the work of unlearning the suppression that has kept you small. It is the slow, deliberate practice of feeling the fire in your belly and knowing that it belongs to you, that it is a necessary part of your wholeness. The body knows how to heal, if only you will let it speak.

## The Subtle Shifts of Safety

When eye contact finally begins to feel safe, the shift in your body is not a sudden explosion of light, but a slow, quiet thawing. It happens in the micro-moments, in the spaces between breaths. You might be sitting across from someone you trust, the afternoon sun casting long, golden shadows across the floor. You meet their gaze, and instead of the familiar jolt of adrenaline, there is a pause. The body hesitates, waiting for the threat, and when it doesn’t come, something profound begins to change.

The first thing you might notice is the softening of the trapezius. Those thick bands of muscle that have spent years hiking your shoulders up toward your ears finally begin to let go. It’s a sensation like ice melting into water - a heavy, warm release that travels down your spine. The levator scapulae, the muscles that hold the tension of every unspoken word, loosen their grip. You can feel the weight of your own bones settling into the chair, the sit bones rooting down into the earth. This is not just relaxation; it is the body remembering how to be held by gravity rather than fighting against it.

As the shoulders drop, the breath changes. For so long, your breath has been trapped in the upper chest, shallow and quick, a bird fluttering against a cage. But now, as the gaze holds and the nervous system registers safety, the diaphragm begins to move freely. You take a breath, and it travels deeper, past the ribs, down into the lower belly. The belly expands, soft and unashamed. This deep, diaphragmatic breathing is the body’s way of signaling to the vagus nerve that the war is over, at least for now. The heart rate slows, the pulse steadying into a calm, rhythmic beat. The body knows this peace.

In this space of safety, the jaw unclenches. The masseter muscles, which have ground your teeth in silent fury for years, finally soften. You might feel a slight parting of the lips, a release of the tongue from the roof of the mouth. The face itself changes, the tiny muscles around the eyes - the orbicularis oculi - relaxing their vigilant squint. You are no longer bracing for impact. You are simply here, present in the moment, allowing yourself to be seen without the armor of tension.

There is a quiet power in this. It is the power of a nervous system that has learned to trust its own capacity for connection. You realize that you do not have to shrink to be safe, that your visibility is not a liability. The body, which has carried the weight of suppression for so long, begins to trust the space it occupies. This is not pathology, this is biology healing itself. It is the slow, deliberate reclamation of your right to exist, to look and be looked at, to hold your ground with a soft belly and an open gaze.

## Reclaiming the Gaze: An Embodied Practice

Find a place where you will not be interrupted. It could be a quiet corner of your living room, the floorboards cool beneath you, or a sturdy wooden chair that holds your weight without complaint. Sit down. Feel the exact point where your sit bones meet the surface beneath you. Notice the pressure, the gravity pulling you downward. This is your anchor. Your body needs to know it is supported before it can risk the vulnerability of looking up.

Take a breath, not a forced, deep inhale, but a slow drawing of air into the lower belly. Feel the diaphragm expand, pushing gently against the waistband of your clothes. Let the exhale be long and slow, a sigh that travels down your spine. Notice the masseter muscles in your jaw. Are they tight? Are your teeth clenched? Deliberately part your lips just a fraction. Let the tongue drop away from the roof of your mouth. The jaw remembers every time you swallowed your words; give it permission to rest now.

Now, bring your attention to your eyes. You don’t need a partner for this first step; a mirror will do, or even a photograph of someone you trust. If you are using a mirror, look at the space just between your own eyebrows. Don’t lock your gaze like a predator. Soften the muscles around your eyes, the orbicularis oculi. Imagine the gaze not as a laser beam piercing outward, but as a wide, receptive net taking the world in. Notice what happens in your body the moment you make contact with your own reflection.

Does the stomach drop? Does the breath catch in the upper chest? Does the sternocleidomastoid muscle in your neck tighten, preparing to turn your head away? Track these sensations without judgment. This is not pathology; this is biology. Your nervous system is simply running its old survival scripts. When you feel the urge to look away, don’t force yourself to stare. That only reinforces the trauma. Instead, let your eyes drift away slowly, intentionally. Look at the floor, the wall, your own hands. Let the nervous system settle.

When you feel ready, bring your gaze back. This time, try to hold it for just one breath longer. Feel the sit bones grounding you. Feel the belly expanding. If the panic rises, remind your body: *I am here. I am safe.* You are teaching your nervous system a new language, one micro-moment at a time. You are unspooling the fascia that has held your fear, releasing the muscles that have braced against connection.

This practice is not about achieving a perfect, unwavering stare. It is about building the capacity to stay in your body while being seen. It is about noticing the urge to flee and choosing, gently, to stay. The body knows how to heal, but it requires your patience. It requires you to honor the tension, to respect the fear, and to slowly, deliberately, invite the gaze back into the light.

## FAQ

**Q:** Why does holding eye contact feel so terrifying, like my throat is closing and my heart is pounding out of my chest?

**A:** The body knows this well. When you hold your gaze, it’s not just your eyes meeting theirs - it’s your whole nervous system stepping into a spotlight it’s been trained to avoid. Your sternocleidomastoid muscles, those thick bands running up the sides of your neck, tense as if bracing against a storm. Your diaphragm tightens, cutting off your breath like a gate slammed shut. This is not pathology; this is biology. The jaw remembers every time you were told to “stop being so angry,” every moment you clenched your teeth to keep your fury inside. That flood of adrenaline racing through your veins, the prickling sweat along your spine, the dry tightness in your mouth - these are your ancient survival responses kicking in. Your body is screaming, “Danger!” because it’s been trained to equate your fury - and the vulnerability of being seen - with risk. This is not weakness; it’s your body’s fierce, protective memory. When the throat closes and the heart pounds, it’s the voice of your body demanding that you pay attention, that you be gentle with yourself as you stretch this muscle of presence.

**Q:** What should I do when my body panics during eye contact, like I want to look away or run?

**A:** When the body panics, it’s giving you a visceral message: you’re in an unfamiliar territory, and your nervous system is trying to protect you. The first thing to do is not to fight this panic, but to listen to it. Feel into the masseter muscle in your jaw; is it clenched so tight it aches? Are your trapezius muscles high and rigid by your ears, like they’re trying to hold your head up against a windstorm? A good herbal ally here is wild oat tincture - sharp, green, and slightly bitter - known for its ability to soothe the nervous system’s overdrive without dulling your awareness. You might take a tiny sip and place your hand over your solar plexus, that soft hollow just below your sternum, feeling its rise and fall with each breath. Breathe slowly into the belly, letting the psoas muscle, that deep core muscle running from your lumbar spine to your femur, soften and release. If your body wants to look away, honor it briefly. Look down toward the soft crease of your elbow or the curve of your wrist where the skin is thin and cool. This allows your system to catch its breath. Then, when you feel a fraction steadier, return your gaze. Eye contact is a muscle itself, and like any muscle, it needs time, patience, and consistent gentle practice to grow stronger.

**Q:** How do I know if I’m pushing myself too hard with this practice?

**A:** The line between growth and overwhelm is not always clear, but the body’s subtle whispers will tell you. If after a session your neck muscles - specifically the splenius capitis and levator scapulae - remain stiff and sore into the next day, or if your jaw stays clenched long after the moment has passed, these are signs you’ve gone too far too fast. Pay attention to the quality of your breath. If it stays shallow, stuck in your upper chest, or if your pulse feels erratic and your sleep becomes restless, your nervous system is still in a state of alarm. You might notice a dull ache behind your eyes or a lingering taste of tension in your mouth like metal or dry earth. This is your body’s way of telling you to slow down, to back off and integrate what you’ve already done. Pushing too hard often means you’re trying to outrun the body’s wisdom, but this is a dance, not a sprint. The body remembers, yes, but it also resists being forced. True progress happens in the spaces where you can feel safety even in discomfort, where you can notice the tension and soften it deliberately, without panic or judgment. Restoring balance means respecting your limits, not ignoring them. When in doubt, turn to gentle herbs like chamomile or lemon balm, steeped hot and sipped slowly in the quiet hour before sleep. Let the warmth settle like a balm over your muscles and nerves, reminding you that this is not a battle but a reclamation - a return to yourself, in your own time.
